content.text: This page is to summarize the safety standards needed for  AI cockpit

rows: Road vehicles – Safety Of The Intended Functionality
Road vehicles — Safety and artificial intelligence
Define ODD, triggering conditions, acceptance criteria
Monitoring data drift, model performance, OOD events

content.text: SOTIF stands for Safety Of The Intended Functionality , defined by the standard ISO 21448 . It addresses hazards that arise not from system failures, but from the limitations of the intended function itself — i.e., when a system works exactly as designed but is still unsafe due to:

items: Performance limitations of sensors, algorithms, or actuators (e.g., a camera blinded by sunlight, radar confused by rain).
Insufficient situational awareness (edge cases the system wasn't designed to handle).
Unintended behavior of AI/ML-based perception or decision-making systems.

content.text: It is especially important for ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) and automated driving (Levels 1–5), where complex algorithms and machine learning are involved

rows: Sensor limits, algorithm gaps, unknown scenarios, misuse
System works as specified — but spec is insufficient
ASIL (QM, A–D) via S × E × C
Acceptance criteria via scenarios (Areas 1–4: known-safe, known-unsafe, unknown-unsafe, unknown-safe)
FMEA, FMEDA, FTA, redundancy, diagnostics, safety mechanisms
Scenario-based testing, statistical validation, ML model validation, simulation, field operation tests
Random hardware failure metrics, fault injection
Reducing unknown/unsafe scenarios via large-scale testing
ADAS, AD, perception systems, AI/ML features

content.text: ISO/PAS 8800 Functional Safety for AI in Vehicles

It is the first international automotive standard specifically addressing the safe development, integration, and lifecycle management of AI/ML components in road vehicles. It bridges the gap between traditional functional safety standards and the unique challenges of data-driven, machine-learned systems
